evals.prompt: Build an active-search interaction with HTMX: typing in the search box should debounce requests and replace only the results list with server-rendered HTML.
evals.expected_output: The response uses HTMX as hypermedia, not as a thin wrapper around JSON. It chooses the right trigger, target, and swap semantics for a server-rendered active-search interaction.
expectations: It returns or expects HTML fragments rather than JSON for the HTMX update.
It uses an HTMX trigger pattern appropriate for debounced search input.
It targets only the results region rather than re-rendering the whole page needlessly.
It chooses a sensible swap strategy for replacing search results.

evals.prompt: Implement delete-row behavior with HTMX so the user confirms the action and the deleted row disappears cleanly without a full page reload.
evals.expected_output: The response uses HTMX interaction semantics intentionally. It picks the right element type, request trigger, and swap behavior for row deletion and keeps the UX semantically sound.
expectations: It uses semantic action elements such as a button for deletion rather than misusing plain links.
It specifies HTMX attributes that update only the affected row or table region.
It handles confirmation in an HTMX-appropriate way.
It keeps the solution server-rendered rather than switching to client-side state management.

evals.prompt: I want a modal loaded via HTMX. Explain the target and swap choices clearly, and tell me what common HTMX inheritance or event mistakes to avoid.
evals.expected_output: The response explains the HTMX mechanics, not just the final markup. It chooses a target/swap strategy that makes modal loading work and calls out common inheritance or event pitfalls.
expectations: It explains the chosen target and swap semantics explicitly.
It calls out at least one relevant HTMX inheritance or event pitfall.
It keeps the guidance in HTMX terms instead of sliding into generic JavaScript modal advice.
It treats the server response as HTML content for the modal.
